coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dot antennas for high efficiency Förster resonance energy transfer based dye-sensitized solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Subramanian, Alagesan
Pan, Zhenghui
Rong, Genlan
Li, Hongfei
Zhou, Lisha
Li, Wanfei
Qiu, Yongcai
Xu, Yijun
Hou, Yuan
Zheng, Zhaozhao
Zhang, Yuegang
coredata.dc:description:
The light harvesting efficiency of an acceptor dye can be enhanced by judicious choice and/or design of donor materials in the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) based dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs). In this work, we explore graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as energy relay antennas for the high power conversion efficiency Ru-based N719 acceptor dyes. The absorption, emission, and time decay spectral results evidence the existence of the FRET, the radiative energy transfer (RET), and a synergistic interaction between GQDs and N719 dye. The FRET efficiency is measured to be 27%. The GQDs co-sensitized DSSC achieves an efficiency (ƞ) of 7.96% with a J
sc of 16.54 mAcm−2, which is 30% higher than that of a N719-based DSSC. GQDs also reduce the charge recombination, which results in an increased open-circuit voltage up to 770 mV. The incident photon-to-current conversion efficiency and UV–Vis absorption measurement reveal that the enhanced absorption of the GQDs antennas is responsible for the improved J
sc in the whole UV–Visible region, while the RET/FRET and the synergistic effect contribute to the significant increase of J
sc in the UV region.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot antenna
Förster resonance energy transfer
Synergistic interaction
Dye-sensitized solar cell

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The financial support provided by CHINESE ACADEMY OF SCIENCES VISITING FELLOWSHIP FOR RESEARCHRS FROM DEVELOPING COUNTRIES (GRAND No. 2014FFJB0006), The National Natural Science Foundation of China (21303251, 21433013 and 1403287), The Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province, China (BK20140383), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2014M550314) and Suzhou Science and Technology Development Program (ZXG2013002) are gratefully acknowledged. We thank Mr. Linjian (Ph.D student), College of chemistry, Suzhou University, Suzhou, for his assistance to conduct time resolved PL spectra.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the as-prepared GQDs. (b) UV–visible absorption spectrum of the GQDs dispersed in ethanol. Inset shows the photographs of the GQDs solution taken under normal light (left) and UV light (right) illumination. (c) Down-conversion PL spectra of the GQDs.

(a) Normalized absorption spectrum of N719 dye and emission spectra of GQDs upon excitation at 310 nm. (b) Emission spectrum of the fixed concentrations of GQDs upon the successive addition of N719 dye. (c) Energy transfer efficiency of GQDs as a function of N719 dye concentration. (d) Time resolved photoluminescence decay measurement (excitation at 450 nm) of acceptor (N719), donor (GQDs) in the presence (optimized concentration) and absence of acceptor, respectively.

(a) Absorption spectrum of the fixed concentrations of the 1 μM N719 dye upon the successive addition of GQDs solution. (b) Absorption spectra of the pure N719 dye, pure GQDs and their mixture solution with the same concentration as that of the pure N719 dye and GQDs. (c) Net changes of dye absorption (Δα) in the presence of GQDs. (d) Relative changes of effective extinction coefficient (Δα/α) of the GQD-N719 couple and sum of individual components.
(a) J–V characteristics curve of the DSSCs. (b) IPCE action spectra of the two photoelectrodes. (c) Nyquist plot and (d) Bode phase plot of the EIS spectra of the DSSCs, respectively.

(a) Energy level diagram and energy transfer process of the GQDs and N719 dyes. (b) Schematic presentation of overall energy transfer, interaction, and electron transport processes in the DSSC system.
J-V characteristics and the values extracted from the EIS spectra of the two electrodes.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dot antennas for high efficiency Förster resonance energy transfer based dye-sensitized solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: i-Lab, Suzhou Institute of Nano-Tech and Nano-Bionics, Chinese Academy of Science, Suzhou, Jiangsu 215123, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Suzhou Institute of Nano-Tech and Nano-Bionics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. i-Lab, Suzhou Institute of Nano-Tech and Nano-Bionics, Chinese Academy of Science, Suzhou, Jiangsu 215123, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Suzhou Institute of Nano-Tech and Nano-Bionics

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The light harvesting efficiency of an acceptor dye can be enhanced by judicious choice and/or design of donor materials in the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) based dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs). In this work, we explore graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as energy relay antennas for the high power conversion efficiency Ru-based N719 acceptor dyes. The absorption, emission, and time decay spectral results evidence the existence of the FRET, the radiative energy transfer (RET), and a synergistic interaction between GQDs and N719 dye. The FRET efficiency is measured to be 27%. The GQDs co-sensitized DSSC achieves an efficiency (ƞ) of 7.96% with a J
sc of 16.54 mAcm−2, which is 30% higher than that of a N719-based DSSC. GQDs also reduce the charge recombination, which results in an increased open-circuit voltage up to 770 mV. The incident photon-to-current conversion efficiency and UV–Vis absorption measurement reveal that the enhanced absorption of the GQDs antennas is responsible for the improved J
sc in the whole UV–Visible region, while the RET/FRET and the synergistic effect contribute to the significant increase of J
sc in the UV region.

ce:section.ce:para: Dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) are currently attracting widespread academic and commercial interests for the conversion of sunlight into electricity with low cost and high efficiency. Excellent photoelectron conversion efficiencies have been reported for regenerative photoelectrochemical cells based on nanocrystalline titanium dioxide (TiO2) films, sensitized by ruthenium dyes using iodide/triiodide (I−/I3 −) redox couple in an organic solvent as the electrolyte [1,2]. The performance of the DSSCs depends on many factors such as the anatase TiO2 surface morphology, electrolyte and nature of the dye. Among many influencing factors, light harvesting efficiency of dyes is one of the key problems, which limits the DSSCs achieving higher photoconversion efficiency. The traditional organic dyes used in DSSCs suffer from narrow absorption spectra or low molar extinction coefficients [3,4]. One way to achieve higher light absorption could be by utilizing Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) phenomena. FRET is a distance dependent radiationless transfer of energy from an excited donor fluorophore (energy donor) to a suitable acceptor dye molecule (energy acceptor). A high FRET efficiency could be obtained when there is a good overlap of the emission spectrum of energy relay dyes (ERDs, energy donor) and absorption spectrum of the energy acceptor, which requires proper band edge alignment between the energy donors and energy acceptors [5,6]. This strict requirement significantly limits the variety of donor and acceptor dyes suitable for the FRET-based DSSCs. The use of ERDs is emerging as a unique approach to improve the efficiency of DSSCs. So far, researches have not been intensively explored to develop an efficient material for high efficiency FRET-based DSSCs. In an attempt to enhance the light absorption, Z907 dye as an acceptor with NaYF4:Er3+/Yb3+ nanoparticles as a donor [7], 5-carboxy-2-((3-(1,3dihydro-3,3-dimethyl-1-ethyl-2H-indol-2-ylidene)methyl)-2-hydroxy-4-oxo-2-cyclobuten-1ylidene)methyl)3-3-dimethyl-1-octyl-3H-indolium (SQ02) dye with Cdse/CdS/ZnS quantum dots (QDs) [8], squaraine dye with CdS QDs [9], SQ-1 dye with 4-(dicyanomethylene)-2-methyl-6-(p-dimethylaminostyryl)-4H-pyran (DCM) in Spiro-OMeTAD based solid-state DSSC [10], zinc phthalocyanine (TT1) with DCM [11], TT1 with Rhodamine B [11], DCM with BL 302 and BL305 [12] have been demonstrated. Even though these ERDs could effectively enhanced the overall performance in these studies, the power conversion efficiency (PCE) was limited (∼3.5%) by the low efficiency of these acceptor dyes used as sensitizer. In some other studies, ERDs have been employed for high PCE acceptor dyes such as Ru based dyes. To date, a high PCE efficiency of 11.1% has been recorded using N719 dye in the Ru-sensitizer based DSSCs [13]. N719 dye with ZnO NP [4], [Ru(dcbpy)2-(acac)]Cl, where acac is acetylacetonato, with Fluorol 7GA [14], N719 dyes with CdSe QDs with mixed amino ligands [15], N719 with CdSe/CdS QDs with core-shell structure [16], N719 with Au@C-dots in ZnO electrode-based DSSCs [17], SQ1 with phenanthroline ruthenium(II) sensitizer (N877) in Spiro-OMeTAD [18] based solid-state DSSCs have been demonstrated. However, these strategies still have drawbacks with regards to lower overall PCE (ƞ) achieved by the DSSC (ƞ ∼ 4%) and/or limited choice of energy donors/acceptors restricted the net improvement of PCE is less than 5% compare to the reference cell. In the past few years, researchers have paid much attention on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) because of its large optical absorptivity, size-dependent bandgap, tunable fluorescence, upconversion emission, hot electron injection, long term photostability, chemical inertness, nontoxic and low cost [19-22]. There are very few reports using GQDs as co-sensitizer for high PCE dyes such as N3 and N719 dyes. Fang et al. [23] combined GQDs and N719 dye. The best result gave a better DSSC efficiency of 6.1% with a net PCE improvement of ∼19% compared with the reference DSSC efficiency of 5.1%. They attributed the enhancement to the photoexcitation and hot electron injection from GQDs to TiO2. They did not study the FRET in the donor-acceptor combination. Mihalache et al. [24] reported an efficiency enhancement of DSSC using N3 dye co-sensitized with GQDs. They achieved an overall cell efficiency of 2.15% compared to that of the reference efficiency 1.92% (net PEC improvement is ∼12%). In general, GQDs have high molar extinction coefficient and wide absorption spectra. Therefore, they could be a good ERD candidate for FRET-based DSSCs.

Here we have demonstrated a facile and effective approach, where GQDs can serve as energy transfer antennas to receive and transmit energy to N719 dye. Moreover, this donor-acceptor couple has exhibited synergistic interaction in addition to FRET, which results in a significant enhancement in the light harvesting efficiency and hence photocurrent generation of the DSSC. A general and reliable model has been proposed to understand the FRET phenomena in terms of the bandgap structure of the N719 dye and GQDs, and the interaction between these two complexes.

Graphite was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, Shanghai. TiO2 nanoparticles (Degussa-P25) were purchased from Germany. All other reagents were of analytical reagent grade and was used as-received. Deionized (DI) water (18.2 MΩ) was used throughout all the experiments.

Graphene oxide (GO) was synthesized from natural graphite powder by modified Hummer's method [25]. An additional preoxidation procedure was performed prior to the GO preparation. The graphite powder (20 g) was added to concentrated H2SO4 (30 ml), K2S2O8 (10 g) and P2O5 (10 g) and heated at 80 °C for 6 h under stirring. The resultant mixture was allowed to cool to room temperature. Then, the mixture was diluted with distilled water (3.5 L), filtered, and washed with distilled water until the water reaches neutral pH. The product was dried in a vacuum drier. The preoxidized graphite was used to prepare GO solution. In brief, 0.5 g of preoxidized graphite was stirred in 12 ml of H2SO4 followed by slow addition of 1.5 g of KMnO4 in an ice-cold water bath (0 °C). The mixture was then stirred at 35 °C for 2 h. Then, 24 ml of distilled water was added drop wise into the mixture. The reaction system was heated again at 80 °C for 30 min and cooled to room temperature. Then, 1.25 ml of H2O2 was added after the drop wise addition of 70 ml of distilled water into the mixture. The resultant product was filtered through microporous membrane and washed with 125 ml of 5 wt% HCl aqueous solution in order to remove metal ions, and then washed with large amount of DI water using centrifugation method. The GO product was suspended in DI water and subjected to dialysis in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 8000 Da) for several days to remove acids and metal ions completely. Then, the GO solution was untrasonicated for 30 min to get homogeneous GO solution. Graphene sheets (GSs) were obtained from chemical reduction of GO. Briefly, 200 μL of N2H4·H2O and 350 μL of NH3·H2O were mixed with 100 ml of 0.5 mg/ml GO solution. The solution mixture was heated at 100 °C for 3 h and cooled to room temperature. The resultant solution was freezed and dried in vacuum drier to obtain GSs.

GQDs were synthesized using chemical reduction method with sodium borohydrate (NaBH4) [26]. In a typical synthesis of GQDs, 50 mg of GSs were mixed with concentrated H2SO4 (10 ml) and HNO3 (30 ml). The mixture was heated and refluxed at 100 °C for 24 h to oxidize the GSs. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was diluted with 250 ml of distilled water, filtered through 0.22 μm microporous membrane and washed with DI water to remove acids. Then, the filter cake was redispersed in 40 ml of DI water. The pH of the solution was tuned to 8 with 1 M NaOH aqueous solution. Then, 0.5 g of NaBH4 was added slowly and stirred at room temperature for 3 h. The suspension was heated upto 85 °C for 16 h (cutting and reduction step). The resulting black suspension was filtered using a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to remove larger sized particles. The GQDs solution was further dialyzed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 3000 Da) for several days to remove the acids and salts. The GQDs dispersed in water was replaced by ethanol using rotary evaporator.

Fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) glasses were cleaned in an acetone and isopropyl alcohol mixture (1:1 v/v) using ultrasonic water bath. TiO2 paste was prepared as follows: 2 ml of DI water and ethanol mixture solution (1:1 v/v) was added to 0.75 g of P25 powder and 0.25 g of polyethylene glycol (molecular weight = 20000 g/mol). The mixture was stirred for 6 h by a magnetic stirrer. Then, 25 μL of 2 M TiCl4 solution was added and stirred again for 12 h to get a high viscous paste. The TiO2 electrodes were prepared by layer-by-layer coating of the TiO2 paste on the FTO glasses using a doctor-blade. Adhesive tape was used to control the thickness of the film and was measured to be ∼13 μm (Fig. S1 in the supporting information). The TiO2 electrodes were annealed at 450 °C for 30 min in air atmosphere. A TiCl4 post-treatment was applied to the sintered electrodes to increase the dye absorption. The TiO2 electrodes were immersed into a 70 mM TiCl4 aqueous solution and kept in an oven at 70 °C for 30 min. After washed with water and drying, the electrodes were sintered again at 450 °C for 30 min.

An active area of 0.4 cm × 0.4 cm was selected from the TiO2 electrodes and immersed in a 3 × 10−4 M of the dye [RuL2(NCS)2]TBA2, where L stands for 2,2’-bipyridyl-4,4’-dicarboxylic acid, NCS represents isothiocyanate and TBA is tetra-n-butylammonium (N719 dye, HANFENG, Shanghai) in ethanol solution, for 16 h. Then, the TiO2 electrodes were washed with ethanol and dried in air (the standard DSSC is designated as ‘N719’). To prepare N719 electrode co-sensitized with GQDs, one of the N719 electrode was immersed in the as-prepared GQDs solution for 12 h (designated as ‘N719/GQD’). Finally, the electrode was washed with ethanol and dried in air. The counter electrode was prepared by spin-coating the 10 mM chloroplatinic acid hexahydrate (H2PtCl6·6H2O) solution (dissolved in ethanol) on the FTO glass substrate, followed by annealing at 350 °C for 1 h. The I−/I3 − electrolyte composed of 0.3 M 1,2-dimethyl-3-propylimidazolium iodide, 0.05 M I2, 0.5 M LiI, and 0.5 M 4-tert-butylpyridine (TBP) in acetonitrile (HANFENG, Shanghai) was cast onto the TiO2 working electrodes. The TiO2 working electrodes and the platinum counter electrodes were clamped together in order to assemble the DSSC devices.

The thickness of the TiO2 film was measured by field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM, HITACHI S-4800). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) was carried out to examine the GQDs sizes using an FEI Tecnai G2 F20 S TEM, operated at 200 kV. Raman spectrum of the GQDs was collected on a Raman spectroscope (HORIBA Scientific) equipped with a CCD detector and confocal microscope using 532 nm laser excitation. The ESCALAB 250Xi (Thermo Scientific) X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) was used to determine the elements present in the as-prepared GQDs. The absorption spectra of the GQDs and N719 were recorded using a UV/vis spectrometer (Lambda 750, PerkinElemer Precisely). The photoluminescense (PL) properties of the GQDs were recorded with HITACHI F-4600 fluorescence spectrometer. Time resolved PL decay spectra were performed using a HORIBA scientific Fluorolog-3 steady state fluorometer with a time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSCP) spectrometer and a pulsed LED lamp as the excitation source. The data were collected and fitted with Datastation and DAS6 software respectively. To examine the chemical coupling between the GQDs and N719, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were taken using JASCO FT/IR-460 plus spectrometer. The photovoltaic characteristics of DSSC devices were measured by an electrochemical workstation (ZAHNER ZENNIUM) under a standard AM 1.5 light illumination (SCIENCETECH SS150 solar simulator, Canada). The light intensity was calibrated to be 100 mW/cm2 using a standard Si solar cell. The electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were also measured in the range of 0.01 Hz–100 kHz using the same equipment and setup. ZVIEW software was used to fit the Nyquist plot of the EIS spectra. The incident photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) was also measured with the same testing system using ZAHNER CIMPS workstation in the wavelength range of 300–800 nm.

Fig. 1 (a) shows the TEM image of the as-synthesized GQDs. The as-synthesized GQDs have very narrow size distribution in the range of 2–7 nm with an average diameter of 4 nm. The as-synthesized samples comprised of well-dispersed and uniformly distributed clusters of GQDs (Fig. S2 in the Supporting Information). The Raman spectrum of GQDs is shown Fig. S3(a) (Supporting Information). The GQDs exhibit two characteristic Raman peaks at 1365 and 1584 cm−1 corresponding to D and G band of graphene respectively [27]. The relative ID/IG ratio for GQDs is 1.09, which is higher than that of the GQDs prepared by other methods [27,28]. This result implies that the surface of GQDs has many structural defects with various oxygenated groups. Oxygen-containing groups are introduced to the edges and onto the basal plane of the GQDs during the oxidation process, which make the GQDs more soluble in water [28,29]. Fig. S3(b) (Supporting Information) shows typical XPS spectrum and its corresponding deconvoluted peaks of the as-prepared GQDs. The peaks are assigned to four main types of carbon bonds [29,30]: graphitic carbon (CC (284.4 eV) and CC (284.9 eV)), and oxygenated carbon (COH (285.7 eV) and COOH (289 eV)), which suggest that the oxygenated functional groups are significantly attached to GQDs.

The optical properties of the GQDs are shown in Fig. 1(b–c). When irradiated with UV light, the as-synthesized GQDs emit violet fluorescence as shown in the inset of Fig. 1(b). It is clearly noticed from the absorption spectrum (Fig. 1(b)) that the as-prepared GQDs show a strong absorption in the UV region and extends its absorption to red region, which could be due to different size distribution of GQDs. The UV–visible spectrum of GQDs shows three absorption bands at ∼280, ∼330 and ∼400 nm. The absorption peak below 300 nm is assigned to π → π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains [31] and the absorption at ∼ 330 nm corresponds to n → π* transition of CO bond [32]. A typical absorption band at around 400 nm indicates a newly opened band gap arising from the triple carbenes at the zigzag edges of the GQDs, which is similar to the reported GQDs by Lin et al. [33]. The absorption onset is at around 450 nm and the bandgap of the GQDs is estimated to be ∼2.75 eV. Fig. 1(c) shows the corresponding emission spectrum of the as-prepared GQDs. The as-prepared GQDs exhibit excitation-dependent PL behaviors. Interestingly, the PL spectrum shows broader peaks in the 300–550 nm region. The PL emission peaks shift to longer wavelengths and the peak intensity gradually decreases with increasing excitation wavelength. The excitation-dependent PL behavior is a common phenomenon observed in carbon-based fluorescent materials. Different PL emission behavior of the GQDs is known to originate from different size, shape and defects [28,29]. In addition to down-conversion PL behavior, up-converted emission peak of GQDs starts from 720 nm excitation (Fig. S4 in the Supporting Information), which is one of characteristic property of the GQDs. The up-converted PL property could be possibly attributed to the multiphoton active process [22,34].

As required for FRET, there is a very good overlap between the absorption spectrum of N719 dye and emission spectrum of GQDs (Fig. 2 (a)), which justify the possibility of energy transfer from GQDs to N719 dye. The calculated Förster radius (Ro) (calculation is given in the Supporting Information), the distance at which the probability of energy transfer is 50%, is 2.2 nm, which also validate the energy transfer from GQDs to N719 dye. In principle, when donors and acceptors are at short distance (typically 1–10 nm) from one another, the donor can transfer its excitation energy to the acceptor through the exchange of a virtual photon [8,16]. On the basis of above consideration, FRET process is expected in this system.

To test the energy transfer between the GQDs and N719 dye, we take the fluorescence spectra of fixed concentration of the GQDs as a function of increasing concentration of the N719 dye (sample preparation details are provided in the Supporting Information) and are displayed in Fig. 2(b). GQDs are fluorescencent material, which exhibit PL emission behavior. The N719 dyes have no fluorescence property. If the emitted energy is not absorbed by the N719 dye, the PL intensity of the GQDs should be the same due to fixed concentration of the GQDs and fixed excitation wavelength (310 nm). The PL intensity of fixed concentration of the GQDs solutions quenches with the successive addition of the N719 dye confirming the resonance energy transfer from GQDs to N719 dye [6]. The energy transfer efficiency from donor to acceptor can be calculated using following relationship [35,36]:
E T E = 1 −
I
D A
I D
where ETE is the energy transfer efficiency, I
DA and I
D are the emission intensity of the GQDs with and without N719 dye, respectively. Fig. 2(c) shows that the ETE increases from ∼6% to ∼ 27% as the amount of N719 increases. In order to confirm the FRET and percentage of energy transfer from GQDs to N719 dyes, the PL decay of the donor is examined from Fig. 2(d). The FRET efficiency is calculated from the PL decay measurement using the following equation [3,4,8]:
E = 1 −
τ
D A
τ D
where E is the FRET efficiency, τ DA and τ D are lifetimes of the donor in the presence and absence of the acceptor, respectively. The effective lifetime is calculated from a three exponential fit of the PL decay curve. The calculated average lifetimes are1.6 ns for acceptor alone
(
τ A
)
,
τ
D A
= 1.9 ns (for optimized mixing concentration of GQD and N719) and
τ D
= 2.4 ns. The donor lifetime is shortened and the acceptor lifetime is prolonged for the system that contains both the donor and acceptor, thus indicating the nonradiative energy transfer from the GQDs to N719 dyes [8,16]. The overall FRET efficiency is found to be E = ∼21%, which slightly differ from the ETE calculated from the PL emission (27%). We have also calculated the FRET efficiency in the presence of TiO2 (Fig. S5 in the Supporting Information). The PL time decay of the donor and acceptor in the presence of TiO2 follows the similar trend as in the absence of TiO2. The calculated average lifetime and FRET efficiency are
τ A
= 1.03 ns,
τ
D A
= 1.26 ns,
τ D
= 1.61 ns and E = ∼22%, which is very close to the value that obtained in the absence of TiO2. This trend could be due to that there is no direct FRET occurs between donor and TiO2, because the band gap of TiO2 is higher (3.2 eV) than the GQDs-donor (∼2.68 eV) so that the energy from the GQDs cannot excite the ground state electrons in TiO2. In an energy transfer process, there is a possibility to transfer energy from the GQDs to the N719 dye by means of radiative energy transfer (RET). Ideally, the higher energy photons absorbed by a donor can be transferred to an acceptor by two path ways via FRET, and/or by RET [3]. Itzhakov et al. [3] has demonstrated the ratio of energy transfer by FRET and RET process in their FRET-based DSSCs through transient fluorescence experiments. The relative energy transfer between the donor and acceptor has estimated to be 70% via FRET and only 12.5% via RET with a recombination loss of 17.5%. Therefore, the energy transfer from GQDs to N719 dye could mainly be attributed to the FRET.

To know the light absorption trend, absorption spectra of fixed concentration of the N719 dye (Supporting Information) with the gradual incorporation of GQDs are taken. From Fig. 3 (a), the amount of light absorption of the fixed concentration of N719 increases upon the successive addition GQDs in the 300–600 nm wavelength region, which indicates that the energy donor serves as an additional light absorber and the light absorption could be enhanced by combination of these materials. To further analyze the light absorption mechanism in this system, absorption spectra of the pure N719 dye, pure GQDs solution, and their mixture solution with same concentration as that of the N719 dye and GQDs alone (Supporting Information) are taken. Absorption of the N719 dye in the presence of the GQDs (Fig. 3(b)) is remarkably improved and the absorption enhancement exceeds sum of the individual component. Fig. 3(c–d) indicate that the net absorption (Δα) and relative changes of effective extinction coefficient (Δα/α) of the N719 dye in the presence of GQDs (calculation details are given in the Supporting Information) reach a maximum value at around 325 nm and the excess absorption is improved by ∼ 80% in the 300–350 nm region and ∼20% in the 350–425 nm region compare to sum of the individual component (Fig. 3(d)).

In a FRET process, the already absorbed energy by the two absorbers redistributes themselves and the absorption intensity of the mixed solution (N719 + GQDs) should be equal to the addition of N719 and GQDs absorption intensity. Therefore, the enhancement in the Δα and Δα/α might arise from chemical coupling between these two materials. In order to understand the result, we have examined the FTIR spectra (Fig. S6 in the Supporting Information) of the GQDs, N719 and their mixture. But, the FTIR spectra reveals that there is no new chemical bond arise between GQDs and N719. The reason for the net enhancement remains unknown to us and requires further in-depth study. We suspect that the excess light absorption could be due to a synergistic effect between the donor-acceptor arising from a weak beneficial molecular interaction of these complexes. The synergistic effect of this donor-acceptor (GQDs-N719) couple contributes to major enhancement of light absorption in the UV region in addition to the FRET.

The J-V characteristics of the cells N719 and N719/GQD are shown in Fig. 4 (a) and Table 1 . The ƞ of the N719 cell is 6.12% with a short-circuit current density (J
sc ) of 12.74 mAcm-2 and open-circuit voltage (V
oc ) of 760 mV. The cell N719/GQD greatly enhances the cell efficiency up to 7.96% with a J
sc of 16.54 mAcm-2 and V
oc of 770 mV. The co-sensitization of GQDs improves both the J
sc and ƞ values by ∼30%. Thus, the improved DSSC efficiency is mostly derived from the increase of J
sc value. The V
oc value is also slightly improved (∼10 mV) by the GQDs. The IPCE measurement (Fig. 4(b)) confirms the trend and consistent with the values obtained in the J-V characteristics. The IPCE value of the GQD/N719 electrode is much higher than that of the N719 electrode in the wavelength range of 300–600 nm. From the above analysis, the GQDs themselves serve as an additional light absorber to enhance light absorption in the 300–600 nm region, which results in the photoexcitation and hot electron injection from GQDs to TiO2 [23]. In addition to this, the FRET/RET and, especially, the synergistic molecular interaction, which occur in the 300–425 nm region as confirmed in the absorption measurement (Fig. 3(c–d)), are responsible for the remarkable enhancement of the IPCE in the UV region.

The charge transfer kinetics in the TiO2 film has been studied using EIS. The response in the frequency regions around 104 (ω1), 103 (ω2), 10 (ω3) and 0.1–1.0 Hz (ω4) in the EIS spectra (Fig. 4(c–d)) are assigned to charge transfer processes at the Pt/electrolyte interface, the TiO2/TiO2 particles interface, the TiO2/dye/electrolyte interface, and the Nernst diffusion within the electrolyte, respectively [37,38]. To estimate the charge transfer resistance (R
ct ) at the dye/TiO2/electrolyte interface from the Nyquist plot, an equivalent circuit model (Fig. S7 in the Supporting Information) has been adopted [39]. The recombination rate constant (K
eff ) at the TiO2/dye/electrolyte interface has been estimated from the middle-frequency (f
max ) of the peak that appears at the 10 Hz region (ω3) of the Bode plot and the electron lifetime in the TiO2 film (τ
eff ) has been calculated using the equation [40,41]: 1/2πf
max . The calculated R
ct , K
eff and τ
eff values are summarized in the Table 1. The co-sensitization of GQDs on the N719 electrode (N719/GQD) increase the R
ct value at the TiO2/dye/electrolyte interface, which resulted in reduced recombination rate, suggesting that effective retardation of the charge recombination reaction between electrons in the conduction band of the TiO2 and the electrolyte [42]. The calculated electron lifetime of the N719/GQD electrode is much longer than that of the N719 electrode. The longer electron lifetime indicates the improved electron transport rate in the film due to the suppression of charge recombination, which would lead to higher charge collection efficiency of the DSSCs. Even though the GQDs have structural defects with various oxygenated groups, which serve as recombination center, the overall electron lifetime of the N719/GQD electrode is increased and the charge recombination is suppressed. This could be ascribed to the following reasons: firstly, incorporation of graphene structure will increase the electron transfer rate [43,44] because the sp2 network provides an effective pathway for electrons transportation, enabling longer electron mean free path and less charge recombination, and thus improves the charge collection efficiency [45,46]; secondly, similar to the N719 dye adsorption on the TiO2 surface via the surface OH sites [47], the presence of COOH and OH groups on the GQDs work as the linkers to enable the GQDs to anchor onto the TiO2 surface, which promote the electron injection efficiency [23]. Since the V
oc value is also related to the electron recombination [48], it is evident that the slightly increased V
oc of the N719/GQD electrode is attributed to the suppression of charge recombination by the attached GQDs on the TiO2 nanoparticles.

To understand the whole mechanism in this DSSCs system, we have estimated the HOMO and LUMO orbital levels of the as-synthesized GQDs from cyclicvoltametry (Fig. S8 in the Supporting Information) and are displayed in the energy level diagram (Fig. 5 (a)). The band alignment of the GQDs perfectly matches with the energy bands of N719 dye as required for FRET to occur. The schematic presentation of energy transfer and electron transport processes in the FRET-based DSSC system is visualized in Fig. 5(a) [7,17]. As shown in Fig. 5(b), the light absorbed by the GQDs is transmitted to the N719 dye via FRET/RET process and there could be a weak molecular interaction between this donor-acceptor couple, which enhances the light absorption efficiency of the system. Meanwhile, the HOMO level of the GQD is located above the conduction band (CB) of TiO2 (Fig. 5(a)) and there is another possibility that direct injection of electron from GQDs to CB of TiO2 nanoparticles takes place, which would also contribute to increased photocurrent in the DSSC. Moreover, the attached GQDs increase the TiO2/dye/electrolyte interface resistance, which inhibits the electron recombination rate leading to slightly higher V
oc value in the DSSC.

We have successfully explored that the GQDs is a suitable donor material for the high PCE acceptor N719 dyes in the FRET-based DSSCs. The GQDs not only transfer the energy to N719 dyes via FRET, but also, exhibits a weak molecular interaction with N719 dye and remarkably enhances the overall light absorption capacity of the system. The attached GQDs on the standard DSSC improves the photocurrent density by 30%, which results in higher overall PCE of the cell compare to that of the standard DSSC. The co-sensitization of GQDs also improves the TiO2/dye/electrolyte interface resistance, which suppress the charge recombination from the CB of TiO2 to electrolyte and lead to a higher V
oc value.

We thank Mr. Linjian (Ph.D student), College of chemistry, Suzhou University, Suzhou, for his assistance to conduct time resolved PL spectra.

ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jpowsour.2017.01.043.
